source.title: Basal ganglia-thalamocortical circuits: parallel substrates for motor, oculomotor, 'prefrontal' and 'limbic' functions.

confidence_rationale: This companion review by the same group gives a more detailed and cautious statement of the segregated circuits hypothesis. It articulates the central theme explicitly: structural convergence and functional integration occurs WITHIN each circuit, not BETWEEN circuits. This supports the three-gate model. However, the authors also candidly acknowledge that the hypothesis rests on comparisons between different sets of animals, that only the motor circuit has been demonstrated as a continuous loop in individual subjects, and that the patchy divergent terminal fields within each circuit complicate simple point-to-point topography. The evidence is therefore rated 'mixed': strong support for the existence of functionally distinct loops, tempered by acknowledged incompleteness of direct within-subject anatomical proof for non-motor circuits. The mixed rating reflects epistemic incompleteness, not anatomical contradiction.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Four parallel BG-thalamocortical circuits are identified: motor (putamen/VLo/SMA-MC), oculomotor (caudate tail/SNr/FEF), prefrontal (caudate body/MD/DLPFC-lateral OFC), and limbic (ventral striatum/MD/OFC-ACC-hippocampus). Integration is argued to occur within, not across, each circuit. Single-cell recordings in behaving animals show strong preservation of functional specificity at each relay, consistent with maintained topographic segregation.
mapping.ree_translation: The four-circuit partition can be compressed to the REE three-gate model: (1) motor circuit = action commitment loop; (2) prefrontal circuit = thought loop / E3 complex; (3) limbic circuit = sensorium/harm-goal loop; (4) oculomotor circuit is architecturally analogous to the motor loop but domain-specific. The 'integration within not between' principle aligns with REE insistence on incommensurable error signals: the loops must not merge their credit signals. Key caveat: the authors note divergence within relay stations (patchy terminal fields) that permits local within-circuit integration, distinguishing from a pure cable-like topology.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The four-circuit model predates SD-005 (z_self/z_world latent split). The REE architecture partially reassigns the oculomotor circuit function to the action commitment loop. The admission that non-motor circuit continuity was inferred rather than demonstrated in individual subjects is a genuine limitation; subsequent double-label tracing and human tractography largely confirmed the loops but also revealed interface convergence zones not discussed here.
mapping.source_context: Progress in Brain Research, 1990. Companion to the Trends Neurosci review. Provides full anatomical details including thalamic relay nuclei for each circuit. Remains the standard reference for the four-circuit parallel model.

confidence_components.notes: High source quality; same authorship as the Trends review but provides more anatomical detail and candor about evidential limitations. Mapping fidelity high for the three-gate compression. Transfer risk low.
